era_believed.statement: The One Ring was forged by Sauron in the fires of Mount Doom to control all other Rings of Power.
Gandalf is one of the five Istari, angelic beings sent by the Valar to Middle-earth to oppose Sauron.
Hobbits are a small, peaceful folk who live in underground homes called hobbit-holes in the Shire.
The Balrog of Moria is a corrupted Maia spirit of fire and shadow that dwells beneath Khazad-dûm.
Gandalf fell with the Balrog into the abyss beneath Khazad-dûm and fought it for ten days on the Endless Stair.
After defeating the Balrog, Gandalf was sent back to Middle-earth as Gandalf the White with greater power.
The Shire is a fertile, peaceful land in the northwest of Middle-earth where Bilbo and Frodo Baggins live.
Sauron is a fallen Maia who serves as the Dark Lord of Mordor and seeks to dominate all of Middle-earth.
The palantíri are seven seeing-stones crafted by the Elves of Valinor that allow communication across vast distances.
Elves are immortal beings who do not age or die of natural causes and pass to the Undying Lands when weary.
Minas Tirith is the White City, the great fortress capital of Gondor built into the side of Mount Mindolluin.
The Ents are ancient tree-herding creatures who are the oldest living beings walking in Middle-earth.
Frodo Baggins carried the One Ring from the Shire to Mount Doom to destroy it in the fires where it was forged.
Sméagol was a hobbit-like creature who was corrupted by the One Ring over five hundred years into the creature Gollum.
The Fellowship of the Ring consisted of nine companions chosen in Rivendell to accompany Frodo on his quest.
Rivendell is the hidden Elven valley ruled by Elrond Half-elven, serving as a refuge and council place.
The Nazgûl are nine mortal kings enslaved by Sauron through the Nine Rings and transformed into wraiths.
The Witch-king of Angmar leads the Nazgûl and cannot be killed by any living man according to prophecy.
Legolas is an Elven prince of the Woodland Realm who joined the Fellowship as its representative archer.
Gimli son of Glóin is a Dwarf warrior who represented the Dwarves in the Fellowship of the Ring.
Aragorn is the heir of Isildur who reclaimed the throne of Gondor as King Elessar after the War of the Ring.
The Mines of Moria are the vast underground Dwarven kingdom of Khazad-dûm, long abandoned after the Balrog awakened.
Lothlórien is a golden-wooded Elven realm ruled by Galadriel and Celeborn, hidden from the outside world.
Galadriel bears Nenya, the Ring of Water, one of the three Elven Rings of Power hidden from Sauron.
Isildur cut the One Ring from Sauron's hand at the end of the Second Age but refused to destroy it.
The Dead Marshes are a haunted swampland where the ghostly images of fallen warriors lie beneath the water.
Mordor is a barren volcanic wasteland enclosed by mountain ranges where Sauron rebuilt his fortress of Barad-dûr.
The eagles of Manwë are great sentient birds who serve as messengers and occasionally intervene in battles.
Gandalf carries the Elven ring Narya, the Ring of Fire, given to him by Círdan the Shipwright upon arrival.
The Paths of the Dead run beneath the White Mountains where the oathbreakers' ghosts dwell awaiting redemption.
Treebeard is the oldest of the Ents, a tree-like creature who has guarded Fangorn Forest since the Elder Days.
The Silmarils were three jewels crafted by Fëanor that captured the light of the Two Trees of Valinor.
Valinor is the Blessed Realm across the sea where the Valar dwell and where Elves may sail at life's end.
Bilbo Baggins found the One Ring in Gollum's cave beneath the Misty Mountains during an unexpected adventure.
Saruman was the head of the Istari order who was corrupted by his use of the palantír of Orthanc.
The sword Glamdring, wielded by Gandalf, was forged in the ancient Elven city of Gondolin in the First Age.
The Prancing Pony is an inn in Bree where Frodo first met the ranger Strider, who was Aragorn in disguise.
Mithril is an extremely rare, lightweight metal mined only in Khazad-dûm that is stronger than any steel.
Bilbo's mithril coat, given to him by Thorin Oakenshield, later saved Frodo from a spear thrust in Moria.
Shelob is a giant spider descended from Ungoliant who dwells in the pass of Cirith Ungol above Mordor.
The White Tree of Gondor grows in the court of the Citadel in Minas Tirith as a symbol of the kingdom.
The Mouth of Sauron is a Black Númenórean who serves as Sauron's ambassador and lieutenant at the Black Gate.
Shadowfax is the lord of all horses in Rohan, a Mearas who allows only Gandalf to ride him.
The Rohirrim are the horse-lords of Rohan who came to Gondor's aid at the Battle of the Pelennor Fields.
Théoden King of Rohan was released from Saruman's influence by Gandalf and led his people into battle.
Éowyn, shieldmaiden of Rohan, slew the Witch-king of Angmar on the Pelennor Fields fulfilling ancient prophecy.
Boromir son of Denethor attempted to take the Ring from Frodo but redeemed himself by dying to protect Merry and Pippin.
Denethor was the Steward of Gondor who was driven to madness and despair by secretly using a palantír.
The Black Gate of Mordor is a massive iron gate guarding the main entrance into Sauron's realm.
Samwise Gamgee carried Frodo up Mount Doom when his master could no longer walk under the Ring's weight.
The Grey Havens is the Elven port from which the ring-bearers sail west to the Undying Lands at the end.
Gandalf's fireworks are legendary displays of Elven fire crafted specifically for celebrations in the Shire.
Tom Bombadil is an enigmatic being who is immune to the power of the One Ring in the Old Forest.
Wormtongue was Saruman's agent who poisoned King Théoden's mind with lies and sorcerous whispers in Edoras.
The Corsairs of Umbar are a fleet of pirates descended from Black Númenóreans who raided Gondor's southern coast.
Anduril, the Flame of the West, was reforged from the shards of Narsil for Aragorn before the War of the Ring.
The Uruk-hai are a stronger breed of Orc created by Saruman that can withstand sunlight without weakening.
Helm's Deep is a great fortress in Rohan where the Rohirrim made a desperate stand against Saruman's army.
The Valar are the angelic Powers who shaped the world of Arda and dwell in Valinor across the Sundering Seas.
Mount Doom is the volcanic forge inside Mordor where Sauron originally crafted the One Ring and where it must be destroyed.
Orcs are corrupted beings bred by Morgoth from captured Elves through torture and dark sorcery in the First Age.
Fangorn Forest is an ancient woodland where the Ents dwell and where Merry and Pippin escaped the Uruk-hai.
The Doors of Durin at the west gate of Moria open only when the Elvish word 'mellon,' meaning friend, is spoken.
The phial of Galadriel contains the light of Eärendil's star and was given to Frodo as protection against darkness.
The seeing-stone of Orthanc showed Saruman visions of Sauron's power that gradually corrupted his will.
Isengard is the fortress complex centered around the tower of Orthanc that Saruman transformed into a war factory.
Morgoth was the first Dark Lord, a Vala who corrupted the world and whom Sauron served as chief lieutenant.
Elrond hosted the Council at Rivendell where the decision was made to destroy the One Ring rather than wield it.
Barad-dûr is Sauron's dark tower in Mordor, held together by the power of the One Ring and destroyed when it was unmade.
The Watcher in the Water is a many-tentacled creature that lurks in the lake before the western entrance to Moria.
Pipe-weed is a tobacco-like plant cultivated in the Shire that hobbits smoke in long-stemmed clay pipes.
The Cracks of Doom are the volcanic vents inside Mount Doom where the One Ring can be unmade in the fires.
Oliphaunts are massive elephant-like war beasts used by the Haradrim in battle against Gondor's armies.
The Arkenstone is a great jewel found beneath the Lonely Mountain that Thorin Oakenshield valued above all treasure.
Smaug was a great fire-breathing dragon who conquered the Lonely Mountain and hoarded the Dwarven treasure for nearly two centuries.
The Battle of Five Armies was fought at the Lonely Mountain between Dwarves, Elves, Men, Orcs, and Wargs.
Wargs are giant intelligent wolf-like creatures ridden by Orcs as mounts in battle across Middle-earth.
Bag End is the comfortable hobbit-hole at the top of the Hill in Hobbiton where Bilbo and Frodo lived.
Elvish lembas bread is a wayfaring food that sustains travelers on long journeys with just a small bite.
Merry and Pippin drank Ent-draughts in Fangorn Forest which caused them to grow unusually tall for hobbits.
The Barrow-downs are ancient burial mounds east of the Shire haunted by wights who trap unwary travelers.
The sword Sting glows blue when Orcs are nearby, serving as an early warning system for its bearer.
Gondolin was a hidden Elven city in the First Age that fell to Morgoth's forces after its location was betrayed.
The Númenóreans were a race of Men given an island kingdom by the Valar and blessed with extended lifespans.
Saruman's voice held a hypnotic power of persuasion that could bend the will of those who listened to him.
The Witch-king rides a fell beast, a great winged creature bred in Mordor as a mount for the Nazgûl.
Cirith Ungol is the treacherous pass above Minas Morgul guarded by Shelob and a tower of Orc soldiers.
The River Anduin is the great river of Middle-earth flowing south from the Misty Mountains past Gondor to the sea.
Dwarves were created by the Vala Aulë from stone before the awakening of Elves and Men in Middle-earth.
The Dimrill Dale lies on the eastern side of the Misty Mountains where the Fellowship emerged from Moria.
Gollum guided Frodo and Sam to Mordor but ultimately caused the Ring's destruction by biting it from Frodo's finger.
Weathertop is a ruined watchtower where the Witch-king stabbed Frodo with a Morgul-blade, nearly turning him into a wraith.
The Horn of Gondor was carried by Boromir and its blast could be heard from great distances across the land.
The Two Towers refer to Barad-dûr in Mordor and Orthanc in Isengard, the twin seats of enemy power.
Elven rope from Lothlórien was given to Sam and possessed the ability to untie itself on command.
The Entwives are the lost partners of the Ents who wandered away and have not been seen since the Second Age.
The Dúnedain are the remnant of the Númenórean people who became rangers guarding the northern lands in secret.
The Palantír of Minas Tirith drove Denethor to despair when Sauron showed him selectively hopeless visions through it.
Gwaihir the Windlord is the greatest of the eagles who rescued Gandalf from Orthanc and later from the Celebdil peak.
The Last Alliance of Elves and Men was the great host that besieged Sauron and cut the Ring from his hand.
Minas Morgul is the corrupted fortress city opposite Minas Tirith, taken by the Nazgûl as their stronghold.
The Woodland Realm of Mirkwood is an underground Elven kingdom ruled by Thranduil, Legolas's father.
Erebor, the Lonely Mountain, contains vast halls of Dwarven craft and treasure reclaimed from the dragon Smaug.
Trolls in Middle-earth turn permanently to stone when exposed to direct sunlight, as Bilbo discovered.
The One Ring makes its wearer invisible to mortal eyes but increasingly visible to Sauron and the Nazgûl.
Lothlórien's mallorn trees have silver bark and golden leaves, and the Elves dwell on platforms high in their branches.
Osgiliath was once the capital of Gondor but was ruined in civil war and later occupied by Mordor's forces.
The Council of Elrond determined that no one could safely wield the One Ring against Sauron without being corrupted.
Círdan the Shipwright is the oldest Elf in Middle-earth who builds the ships that sail to the Undying Lands.
The Haradrim are the Men of the South who allied with Sauron and rode Oliphaunts into battle at Pelennor Fields.
Gandalf broke the Bridge of Khazad-dûm beneath the Balrog's feet with his staff, sacrificing himself for the Fellowship.
The Ent-moot was a deliberative gathering of Ents that lasted for days before deciding to march on Isengard.
The Witch-king shattered Gandalf's staff at the gates of Minas Tirith during the siege of the city.
Narsil was the sword of Elendil that broke when he fell fighting Sauron, and its shards were kept in Rivendell.
The three Elven Rings are Narya, Nenya, and Vilya, borne by Gandalf, Galadriel, and Elrond respectively.
Radagast the Brown is a fellow Istari who became devoted to animals and plants rather than opposing Sauron directly.
The Undying Lands lie across the western sea and can only be reached by the straight road after Númenor's downfall.
Samwise Gamgee was elected Mayor of the Shire seven times after returning from the quest to destroy the Ring.
The Argonath are two colossal stone statues of Isildur and Anárion that guard the river approach to Gondor.
Mithrandir is Gandalf's name among the Elves, meaning 'Grey Pilgrim' in the Sindarin tongue of Middle-earth.
